frameworks.name: Top-Down Business Launch Sequence
frameworks.definition: Start with a high-price, limited-volume premium offering; use early profits and brand equity to fund progressively higher-volume, lower-price tiers; never enter from the bottom and attempt to move up.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that building a business from the top down — starting with a high-priced, limited-supply premium offering before scaling to mass-market — is the most effective launch sequence because it anchors brand perception at a premium level that a low-price-first entry can never replicate.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: applies to product or service businesses where brand perception affects willingness-to-pay at lower tiers
claims.mechanism: When a brand launches at a high price, subsequent lower-priced tiers are framed as affordable access to a premium product; when a brand launches at a low price, attempts to introduce premium tiers are not credible because the anchor is already set at the bottom.
bounded_by: does not apply to commodity markets where brand narrative carries no pricing power

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that the brand narrative for a top-down launch sequence — 'many of you couldn't afford our premium product, so we made a more affordable version' — is credible and persuasive, while the reverse narrative — 'we are a budget discounter now selling a premium product' — does not resonate with buyers.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: consumer or prosumer markets where perceived brand status influences purchase decisions
claims.mechanism: Buyers use a brand's established price anchor as a signal of quality; a high anchor transfers positive quality association downward to lower-priced tiers, while a low anchor cannot transfer upward because it lacks the quality signal.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that starting at the premium tier is operationally necessary for early-stage businesses because the volume required to be profitable at mass-market price points demands capital and operational infrastructure that most founders do not have at launch.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: early-stage businesses without pre-existing capital or operational infrastructure
claims.mechanism: Mass-market profitability requires extraordinary volume at razor-thin margins; without the capital to build infrastructure that truly scales, founders who attempt mass-market entry end up trying to squeeze maximum revenue from low-paying customers rather than building sustainable unit economics.
bounded_by: does not apply to well-capitalized businesses that can absorb the operational build-out cost upfront

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that high-end market segments offer more profitability per unit sold compared to mass-market segments, making them the correct starting point for businesses that lack the volume to make thin-margin models work.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: businesses at early stage without established operational scale

examples.text: Alex Hormozi uses Tesla as the canonical illustration of the top-down business launch sequence. Tesla launched with the Roadster — a $250,000 car with very limited production and high profitability per unit. Tesla then moved to the Model S, a still-premium but higher-volume vehicle, and subsequently to the mass-market Model 3 and Model Y. This sequence allowed Tesla to anchor its brand at the highest quality tier, build operational capacity progressively, and frame every successive car as 'an affordable version of our premium product' rather than as a budget vehicle. Hormozi notes that the reverse — starting as a budget manufacturer and then trying to sell a $250,000 car — would not carry the same brand credibility.

practices.text: When launching a new business or product line, begin with the highest-price, lowest-volume tier you can credibly deliver; only expand to higher-volume, lower-price tiers after establishing brand equity and operational capacity.
Avoid entering a market at the budget or mass-market tier if you intend to offer a premium product in the future; the low-price anchor is very difficult to overcome once set.
